real_meaning: ～ながら expresses that two actions happen simultaneously, with the main action (the final verb) being the 'primary' one and the ながら verb being the background activity. The speaker is framing one action as the setting or backdrop for another — like 'while doing A, (mainly) B.' There's an important secondary use where it expresses a concession ('even though / despite'), carrying a slight sense of tension or irony between the two clauses.

formation.rule: Attach ながら to the <ruby>連用形<rt>れんようけい</rt></ruby> (masu-stem) of a verb. For the simultaneous meaning: <ruby>歩<rt>ある</rt></ruby>きながら (while walking), <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>みながら (while drinking). For the concessive meaning it can attach to adjectives or nouns too: <ruby>残念<rt>ざんねん</rt></ruby>ながら (regrettably / despite regret), <ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>りながら (even knowing that).
formation.common_mistake: Learners often attach ながら to the dictionary form instead of the masu-stem. Wrong: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるながら ✗. Correct: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べながら ✓.

sentences.jp: <ruby>音楽<rt>おんがく</rt></ruby><ruby>聴<rt>き</rt></ruby>きながら<ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>してる。
sentences.translation: I'm doing homework while listening to music.
sentences.notes: The dropped particles and ～てる contraction make this sound natural in everyday speech; <ruby>音楽<rt>おんがく</rt></ruby>を<ruby>聴<rt>き</rt></ruby>きながら frames listening as the background activity to the main task of homework.

sentences.jp: <ruby>コーヒー<rt>こーひー</rt></ruby>を<ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>みながら、<ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>話<rt>はなし</rt></ruby>をゆっくり<ruby>聞<rt>き</rt></ruby>きました。
sentences.translation: I listened slowly to what she had to say while drinking coffee.
sentences.notes: ながら here sets a relaxed, unhurried scene — the coffee-drinking is background atmosphere, and <ruby>聞<rt>き</rt></ruby>くことが<ruby>主<rt>しゅ</rt></ruby>な<ruby>行動<rt>こうどう</rt></ruby> (listening is the main action).

sentences.jp: <ruby>誠<rt>まこと</rt></ruby>に<ruby>残念<rt>ざんねん</rt></ruby>ながら、ご<ruby>要望<rt>ようぼう</rt></ruby>にはお<ruby>応<rt>こた</rt></ruby>えできかねます。
sentences.translation: Regrettably, we are unable to meet your request.
sentences.notes: This is the concessive / formulaic use of ながら — it does NOT mean 'while'; it expresses 'despite this feeling of regret' and is a fixed polite expression used in business refusals.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>悪<rt>わる</rt></ruby>いと<ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>りながら、それを<ruby>続<rt>つづ</rt></ruby>けていた。
sentences.translation: He kept doing it, even knowing it was wrong.
sentences.notes: This concessive ながら attaches to a verb (<ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>り) rather than a noun, conveying irony or moral tension — 'despite knowing better, he still did it' — which surprises learners who only know the simultaneous meaning.

confused_with.contrast: Both can translate as 'while,' but they work differently. ながら requires the SAME subject performing both actions at once, and the actions genuinely overlap in real time. ～<ruby>間<rt>あいだ</rt></ruby>（に）describes a time window during which something happens, and the two actions can have different subjects. Minimal pair: <ruby>音楽<rt>おんがく</rt></ruby>を<ruby>聴<rt>き</rt></ruby>きながら<ruby>勉強<rt>べんきょう</rt></ruby>した (I studied while listening to music — same person, simultaneous) vs. <ruby>音楽<rt>おんがく</rt></ruby>を<ruby>聴<rt>き</rt></ruby>いている<ruby>間<rt>あいだ</rt></ruby>に、<ruby>弟<rt>おとうと</rt></ruby>が<ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>ってきた (While I was listening to music, my brother came in — different subject, event happening inside a time window). Use ながら when YOU are doing both; use ～<ruby>間<rt>あいだ</rt></ruby>（に）when you want to talk about what happened during a period, possibly involving someone else.

bunpro_tip: Picture ながら as a movie scene where one action is always in the background shot — the camera (main verb at the end) is focused on the real action, but ながら is the ambient detail. When BunPro gives you a concessive sentence, watch for emotion or knowledge words like <ruby>残念<rt>ざんねん</rt></ruby> or <ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>る as a signal that it's the 'even though' flavor, not the 'simultaneously' one.
